facts.claim: PEP 701 proposes a formalized grammar for f-strings, integrating it directly into the parser and lifting restrictions originally formulated in PEP 498.
The proposed syntactic formalization provides considerable advantages for end users and library developers while dramatically reducing the maintenance cost of f-string parsing code.
The new grammar allows f-string expression components to be defined as "any applicable Python expression," eliminating previous obscure limitations.
The expression component of f-strings can now include any string literal, enabling nesting of string literals (formatted or not) with the same quote type.
All previous restrictions on f-string literals, such as disallowing same-quote strings, backslashes, newlines, and comments, are lifted by the new grammar.
PEP 701 introduces no backwards incompatible syntactic or semantic changes to the Python language.
The PEP simplifies teaching f-strings, as the rule becomes "you can place any valid Python expression inside an f-string expression."
The previous f-string implementation in CPython had high maintenance costs due to hand-written parsing code and could not leverage the new PEG parser's improved error messages.
Other Python implementations lacked a formal grammar for f-strings, making correct implementation and verification difficult.
The PEP builds on the new Python PEG Parser (PEP 617) to redefine f-strings with a clear separation of string and expression components.
Backslashes are now allowed within f-string expression components, resolving a common unintuitive limitation.
New lines and comments (using the # character) are now allowed within f-string expression parts.
The PEP specifies a lower bound of 5 levels of f-string nesting, though implementations are free to impose their own limits.
The new grammar preserves the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) of the current implementation, ensuring no semantic changes to existing f-string code.
Three new tokens (FSTRING_START, FSTRING_MIDDLE, FSTRING_END) are introduced and must be produced by the lexer when processing f-strings.
The `tokenize` module will be updated to emit these new f-string tokens, allowing tools to leverage the new tokenization schema.
Allowing quote reuse and backslashes simplifies code generators by enabling regular Python expression logic inside f-strings without special treatment.
The PEP decided not to reject quote reuse at the parser level, recommending linters and code style tools handle readability concerns instead.
Limiting quote reuse would have significantly increased the complexity of the proposed implementation due to parser context requirements.
F-strings were originally introduced without a formal grammar, leading to restrictions imposed to avoid modifying the existing lexer.
Previous attempts (PEP 536) to lift f-string limitations were never implemented.
Arbitrary nesting of expressions without escape sequence expansion is a feature present in string interpolation methods of many other languages (e.g., Ruby, JavaScript, C#).
The formal handling of f-string debug expressions (e.g., `f"{1+1=}"`) requires the lexer to keep track of raw string contents of the expression part.
Some users find quote reuse within the same string confusing and hard to read, violating a simple Python string delimitation rule.
Concerns were raised that quote reuse might break existing lexer and syntax highlighting tools that rely on simpler parsing mechanisms.
Many other popular languages allow quote reuse in string interpolation, which could make Python more familiar to users from those languages.
The `tokenize` module update represents a minor incompatibility for tools that rely on its previous f-string tokenization behavior.
Expressions allowing a colon (':') character at the top level within an f-string still need to be enclosed in parentheses to avoid being interpreted as a format specification.
The PEP decided not to lift the restriction that some expressions (e.g., lambdas) need parentheses at the top level if they contain ':' or '!'.
The PEP decided to disallow (for the time being) using escaped braces (\{ and \}) in addition to the `{{` and `}}` syntax.
